1: A shadow: cast your bread upon the waters — generous investment without visible return — and you will find it after many days. The image anticipates Christ's own pattern: his death is the bread cast into the waters of death, and the resurrection is its unexpected return. John 12:24 makes the connection explicit: 'unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies, it remains alone; but if it dies, it bears much fruit.' What the Preacher offers as wisdom, the cross enacts as history.
2: A theme: give a portion to seven, even to eight — generous distribution despite uncertainty about what calamity may come. This counsel of generous risk in the face of unknown disaster is fulfilled in God's own action in the gospel: 'He who did not spare his own Son but gave him up for us all — how will he not also, along with him, graciously give us all things?' (Romans 8:32). The liberal distribution of the gospel to all peoples, seven and eight, flows from that ultimate act of costly generosity.
3: A revelation of God: natural processes (rain, a falling tree) follow their own logic and cannot be redirected by human preference. The Creator's governance of creation operates with a consistency that does not bend to wishful thinking. Christ appeals to the same logic — 'he causes his sun to rise on the evil and the good, and sends rain on the righteous and the unrighteous' (Matthew 5:45) — to ground the call to love enemies: the Creator does not calibrate creation's gifts by human merit, and neither should those who follow him.
4: A theme: paralysis through waiting for perfect conditions — watching every wind and cloud — guarantees that neither sowing nor reaping happens. The NT applies this insight to the mission of the church: 'Go therefore and make disciples of all nations' (Matthew 28:19) is a command that overrides the endless waiting for the right moment. Paul's principle — 'whether in season or out of season' (2 Timothy 4:2) — is the apostolic form of the Preacher's counsel: sow without waiting for favorable conditions.
5: A revelation of God: as you cannot know the path of the wind or the formation of bones in the womb, so you cannot know the work of God who makes everything. The hiddenness of God's creative work is one of Ecclesiastes' recurring themes. Christ's response to this mystery is not to deny it but to offer revelation: 'No one knows the Father except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him' (Matthew 11:27). The hidden work of God is made visible in Christ, who reveals the Father (John 14:9).
6: A theme: sow in the morning and do not rest in the evening — diligent work without knowing which effort will succeed. This describes the life of faith in the absence of guaranteed outcomes. Paul's apostolic labor operates precisely on this principle: 'I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth' (1 Corinthians 3:6-7). The sower who goes out to sow in Christ's parable (Matthew 13:3-9) does not know which ground will receive the seed; he sows everywhere, trusting the harvest to God.
7: A revelation of God: light is sweet and it is good for the eyes to see the sun. The Preacher affirms creaturely existence as genuinely good — to be alive and able to see is a gift. Christ claims this imagery for himself: 'I am the light of the world; whoever follows me will not walk in darkness, but will have the light of life' (John 8:12). The physical light that the Preacher calls sweet is the image of the uncreated Light that entered the world to give it permanent day.
8: A theme: enjoy many years, but remember that the days of darkness will be many — all that comes is vapor. The realistic frame around human joy: death awaits, and all earthly pleasure dissolves into hevel. Christ's resurrection breaks this frame without denying it. Death no longer has dominion over him (Romans 6:9), and for those who are in Christ, the 'days of darkness' are not the final word: 'whoever believes in him will not perish but have eternal life' (John 3:16). The vapor becomes permanent in resurrection.
9: A revelation of God: rejoice in your youth — but know that God will bring you into judgment. This is the key verse of the chapter: the summons to enjoy life is qualified by the absolute seriousness of divine accountability. The NT does not soften this but personalizes it: 'God has fixed a day on which he will judge the world in righteousness by a man whom he has appointed; and of this he has given assurance to all by raising him from the dead' (Acts 17:31). The resurrection is the proof that judgment is not theoretical — it has a face.
10: A theme: banish anxiety from your heart; childhood and youth are vapor. The call to set anxiety aside echoes in Christ's own teaching: 'Do not be anxious about your life' (Matthew 6:25); 'Cast all your anxiety on him, because he cares for you' (1 Peter 5:7). The Preacher's counsel is grounded in honest realism — youth passes; the NT grounds it in a Person: anxiety about transience is answered not by denial but by the risen Christ who holds the future.
